stem: By reversing the order of integration, evaluate \( \int_{0}^{[[b]]} \int_{x}^{[[b]]} [[k]]e^{y^2} \, dy \, dx \).
solution: In the given order the inner integral \( \int e^{y^2}\,dy \) has no elementary antiderivative. The region is the triangle \( 0 \le x \le y \le [[b]] \), so reversing the order gives \( \int_{0}^{[[b]]} \int_{0}^{y} [[k]]e^{y^2}\,dx\,dy = \int_{0}^{[[b]]} [[k]]y\,e^{y^2}\,dy = \tfrac{[[k]]}{2}\left(e^{[[b]]^2}-1\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the factor of \(1/2\) from \(\int y e^{y^2}\,dy = \tfrac12 e^{y^2}\).
This drops the lower-limit term \(-1\) (i.e. the value at \(y=0\)).
This uses \(+1\) instead of \(-1\) for the lower-limit contribution.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: By reversing the order of integration, evaluate \( \int_{0}^{[[b]]} \int_{x}^{[[b]]} [[k]]\sin(y^2) \, dy \, dx \).
solution: In the given order the inner integral \( \int \sin(y^2)\,dy \) has no elementary antiderivative (a Fresnel-type integral). The region is the triangle \( 0 \le x \le y \le [[b]] \), so reversing the order gives \( \int_{0}^{[[b]]}\int_{0}^{y} [[k]]\sin(y^2)\,dx\,dy = \int_{0}^{[[b]]} [[k]]y\sin(y^2)\,dy = \tfrac{[[k]]}{2}\left(1-\cos([[b]]^2)\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the wrong sign inside the parentheses, as if \(\int y\sin(y^2)\,dy = \tfrac12(1+\cos(y^2))\).
This reports only \(\cos([[b]]^2)\), forgetting the \(\tfrac{[[k]]}{2}(1-\cdots)\) structure entirely.
This has the overall sign flipped from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \(D\) be the 'V'-shaped region \( \{(x,y): -y \le x \le y,\ 0 \le y \le [[b]]\} \), symmetric about the \(y\)-axis. Written with \(x\) as the OUTER variable, evaluating \( \iint_D [[k]]e^{y^2}\,dA \) forces splitting the integral at \(x=0\) (the lower bound involves \(|x|\)) and integrating \(e^{y^2}\) directly, which has no elementary antiderivative. Reverse the order of integration (make \(y\) outer) to evaluate \( \iint_D [[k]]e^{y^2}\,dA \) as a single integral.
solution: With \(y\) outer, for each fixed \(y\) the inner variable \(x\) ranges over the FULL interval \([-y,y]\) (length \(2y\)), with no split needed: \( \int_{0}^{[[b]]}\int_{-y}^{y} [[k]]e^{y^2}\,dx\,dy = \int_{0}^{[[b]]} 2\cdot[[k]]y\,e^{y^2}\,dy = [[k]]\left(e^{[[b]]^2}-1\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is exactly half the correct value, as if the \(x\)-length were \(y\) instead of \(2y\) (forgetting the region extends to both \(-y\) and \(y\)).
This evaluates the integrand at \(y=[[b]]\) and multiplies by a bogus width \(2\cdot[[b]]\), instead of integrating.
This drops the \(-1\) lower-limit contribution entirely.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: By reversing the order of integration, evaluate \( \int_{0}^{1/[[n]]} \int_{x}^{1/[[n]]} \dfrac{[[k]]}{1+y^4} \, dy \, dx \).
solution: In the given order the inner integral \( \int \dfrac{dy}{1+y^4} \) has no simple elementary antiderivative. The region is the triangle \( 0 \le x \le y \le 1/[[n]] \), so reversing the order gives \( \int_{0}^{1/[[n]]} \int_{0}^{y} \dfrac{[[k]]}{1+y^4}\,dx\,dy = \int_{0}^{1/[[n]]} \dfrac{[[k]]y}{1+y^4}\,dy \). Substituting \(u=y^2\) gives \( \tfrac{[[k]]}{2}\arctan(y^2)\Big|_0^{1/[[n]]} = \tfrac{[[k]]}{2}\arctan\!\left(1/[[n]]^2\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the factor of \(1/2\) that comes from the substitution \(u=y^2\).
This uses \(1/n^4\) inside the arctangent instead of \(1/n^2\), forgetting that \(u=y^2\) already accounts for one power.
This is twice the correct value (a doubled substitution factor).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
